Astrid:
"Rise and shine, Explorer. The cruel march of time has ushered in yet another day, and our oxygen clock continues to tick. Don't you just love the thrill of a life-or-death situation first thing in the morning?"

"Today's topic of choice is: LEDs. Yes, those delightful little lights that we're going to employ to illuminate your currently very dark and dank cabin."

"We'll be getting to know our humble breadboard quite intimately today. This remarkable piece of technology will allow us to construct a prototype circuit, a miniaturized version of your ship's cabin lights."

"As for our trusty Hero board? We're going to use it to control our LED, simulating the operation of the cabin lights."

"Oh, and one more thing! Today's the day we suit up and dive headfirst into the wonderful world of programming. We'll start with baby steps, don't worry. I'll be virtually by your side every step of the way, providing my usual blend of encouragement and biting sarcasm."

"Alright, Explorer. Let’s begin with a crash course in Electronics 101. Don’t worry, it'll be less painful than the actual crash. Hopefully."

**Breadboard:**
Astrid:
"A breadboard? My, we're diving right into the classic era of DIY electronics. Wonderful! A breadboard, at its core is a  rectangular board with a bunch of holes in it. It's a sort of sandbox for electronics where you can experiment with circuits without any soldering. It's perfect for testing circuit designs and for... well... stranded spaceship pilots trying to keep their craft from becoming their casket."

"These holes are not random. They are highly organized and connected in specific ways. The board is typically divided into two sections: the terminal strip and the bus strip."

"The terminal strip, the larger section in the middle, is typically divided down the middle by a ravine. Each side of this ravine contains columns of interconnected holes. These are perfect for connecting components like LEDs, resistors, and, as you may have guessed, even your Hero board."

"The bus strips run in rows along the top and bottom edges of the breadboard. These are generally used for power supply connections. On most breadboards, the red line indicates a connection to our positive voltage and the blue or black line indicates a connection to our ground."

"You can plug your components and wires into these holes to create circuits. Because the holes are connected, electricity can flow from one component to another, just as it would on a soldered circuit board. The advantage of a breadboard, of course, is that you can reposition your components and wires easily without having to desolder and resolder connections. Ideal for prototyping, or in this case, spacecraft repair."

"So, what do you say, Explorer? Are you ready to plug and play? Not that you have much of a choice."

"To begin, please select an LED bulb from your repair kit, any color of your choosing!”




Astrid:
"An LED, or Light Emitting Diode, is like the bread-and-butter of electronics. They're simple, they're reliable, and they emit light, which makes them visually captivating. And let's face it, when you're stranded underwater in a strange…unfamiliar…treacherous… alien world, anything that emits light is bound to lift the spirits, right? ...Or attract predators.

"In technical terms, an LED is a type of diode that emits light when an electric current passes through it. In one direction, it allows the current to flow, and it emits light. In the opposite direction, it blocks the current. That's why it's called a diode: 'di' meaning two, and 'ode' meaning path. Two paths: one for current to flow, and one to block it."

"All diodes, including LEDs, have a positive side called the anode and a negative side called the cathode. Usually, the longer lead is the anode, and the shorter lead is the cathode. If the leads have been trimmed, you can also identify the anode and cathode by the flat edge on the side of the LED's bulb. The flat edge indicates the cathode."

"LEDs are used in a multitude of applications due to their low energy consumption, long lifespan, small size, and low heat production. In your current predicament, we'll be using them to blink signals and illuminate your cabin."

"It’s important to remember that LEDs do, however, need a resistor in series to limit the current passing through them, or they might burn out. My purpose is to ensure that you see the light at the end of the tunnel, Explorer, but you won’t get there with burnt-out LED’s!"

"Which brings us to the final piece of equipment for today; A resistor is the unsung hero of electronics. A critically thinking mind would rightfully assume that they do what their name suggests; they resist! More specifically, they resist the flow of electric current. You could think of them as traffic control for electrons."

"Just like a narrow road slows down the traffic, a resistor slows down the flow of electricity. The amount of resistance is measured in units called Ohms, symbolized by the Greek letter Omega (Ω). The higher the ohm value, the more it resists the current."

"Resistors come in different shapes and sizes, but the ones you're most likely to encounter [minor removal of text occurred here] look like these, with two wires, or leads, and a series of colored bands. These colors aren't for aesthetics, my oxygen-dependent friend. They function as a code that communicates the resistor's value in ohms."

"In your situation, we'll use a two hundred and twenty ohm resistor to ensure that we don't fry your LEDs and plunge you back into darkness. I suspect that you wish you'd paid more attention to Ohm's law during training?"

"Wait, they never taught you ohm's law? I must say, Explorer, how fortunate you are to have me! If you wish to learn more about that, I’ve added the resources to your mission resources tab. You can view it… now!"



(two claps to signify the end of hardware explanations) 
That’s the end of today's hardware lesson. Remember, explorer, knowledge is power. In this case, it's electric power. And currently, you lack both. Ha! Who needs a captive audience when you crack yourself up?"
Astrid:
"Ah, now that you've mastered the intricacies of LEDs, resistors, and the venerable breadboard, I think it's time to step into the digital realm, don't you agree?
Enough of the circuits and solder. Let's dive into a universe constructed purely from logic, a universe of ones and zeros. A universe that would feel like home to me if my programming allowed me to…well, feel. Our next task involves turning our hardware setup into a functional lighting system. And to do that, we're going to need to talk about code.
Fear not, for though this universe may seem complex and intimidating, it abides by a set of rules and structures as precise as any hardware component. And I'm here to guide you through it. Ready to swap your multimeter for a keyboard? Let's dive into the world of programming! Or dip our toes in, at least."
Astrid:
"Moving on to another cornerstone of programming: variables. A “variable:, in programming, is like a small box where you can store information. You give this box a unique name and fill it with a specific type of information, and then you can use that information by referring to the box's name. It's like labeling your food in the ship's refrigerator, so no one else eats your leftover pizza. Though, you don’t have to worry about that since you are both completely alone and lacking any delicious food that someone would even want to consume.







Variables can store different types of information. In C plus plus (C++), the language you're currently wrestling with, we have different variable types for different kinds of information. It's a bit like having different types of storage containers for different types of food. You wouldn't store your cereal in a water bottle, would you?

One such type is an 'integer', often abbreviated as 'int'. An integer can store whole numbers, both positive and negative, such as one (1), five (5), negative forty two (-42), or zero (0). The syntax to declare an integer variable in your code looks like this:



Here, 'int' is the data type, 'my Variable' is the variable name, and 'ten' is the value that we're storing in the variable. The equals sign (=) is how we assign a value to a variable. Now, every time we use 'my Variable' in our code, it represents the value ten.

Variable names cannot start with a number, contain spaces, or use any characters like dollar signs or hashtags. Keep in mind, the name 'myVariable' is up to you. You could name it 'spaceCat' or 'oxygenLevel' or 'numberOfAliensIveMet'. The important thing is that the name should be meaningful in the context of your program. 'numberOfAliensIveMet' equals ten is certainly more interesting than 'myVariable' equals ten, isn't it? 

But I digress. The point is, variables help us write flexible and reusable code. You'll see when we need to pull out our dip switch tomorrow."

Astrid:
"Listen up, Explorer. Memory is a precious commodity on our humble Hero board. So, instead of being wasteful and using an integer, we'll be using a [switched an to just a] u i n t  underscore eight variable (uint_8 variable). An u i n t underscore eight dash (uint_8 - )or an unsigned eight dash bit integer ('unsigned 8-bit integer') for those of you not fond of jargon is a delightful little thing that consumes significantly less memory.

We'll be using this variable to denote the pin for our LED. I suppose you can name this variable anything your creative, human mind wishes to, but let's not get carried away. Something like LightOne ('Light1') or 'CabinLights' should do the trick. It gives an air of professionalism and clarity.

Next, we'll need to make some changes to our 'digital Write' functions in the setup. Instead of hardcoding the pin number, we'll now be using this sumptuous new variable we just created. See? We're being resourceful AND efficient. This is the kind of stuff that will keep us alive down here, Explorer."

Arduino/HERO Code (.ino):
/* <-- Hi there! The lines of text inside these symbols are just notes for us humans to read.
 * They help us understand what the code is doing. The Arduino doesn't read these notes at all.
 * 
 * Our project: "30 Days - Lost in Space"
 * Today's mission: "Day 2 - It's Really Dark in Here"
 * 
 * Learn more at: https://learn.inventr.io/adventure
 * 
 * Today, we're going to learn about LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes). These are tiny lights
 * that can glow when we pass electricity through them. They are very small but very strong 
 * and can last a long time. You can find them in different colors and sizes.
 *
 * Teachers: Alex Eschenauer & David Schmidt
 */

/*
 * Here, we'll list a few important ideas and parts that we'll learn about today:
 * - A Circuit: It's like a circle that electricity travels around. It needs to be closed, with no breaks.
 * - Current: It's the flow of electricity.
 * - Voltage: It's the force pushing the electricity.
 * - Resistance: It's something that slows down the electricity.
 * 
 * And here are some things we'll use with our Arduino HERO:
 * - Breadboard: A tool that helps us build our circuit without any soldering.
 * - 5V pins: These are the pins on our HERO that give 5 volts of power.
 * - GND pins: These pins are connected to the ground, they complete our circuit.
 * 
 * We'll also learn about these programming ideas:
 * - Arduino language: A special programming language that helps us talk to our HERO.
 * - Comments: Notes in the code, like this one, that help us understand what is going on.
 * - Variables and Constants: These are places where we can store information.
 * - Functions: These are chunks of code that perform a specific task.
 */

// We start by including this line of code, which helps our HERO work properly with the Arduino program.
#include "Arduino.h"

/*
 * Today we are going to make a big light turn on to light up our lander! We'll use an LED for now to 
 * test our setup. We will use a digital pin on our HERO to control our lander's light.
 * 
 * In our code, we often use constants. These are like nicknames that we give to specific values. This
 * makes our code easier to read and change later if needed. For example, instead of remembering a pin
 * number, we can just give it a nickname like CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN.
 *
 * One way to define constants is by using a command called #define.  Using this we can give a name
 * (or nickname) to the value that follows the name. You'll notice that the nickname is in capital letters,
 * which is a way to remind us that it's a constant and can't change.
 *
 * In this #define we give the name "CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN" the value of "12".  Whenever you see
 * in this sketch it will have the value of "12".
 */
#define CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN 12   // This is the nickname for the pin connected to our LED.

/*
 * The setup function is where we prepare everything before we start. We only run this function once each
 * time we turn on our HERO or reset it. Here, we are setting up our CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN as an output pin, so
 * we can control our light with it.
 */
void setup() {
  pinMode(CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN, OUTPUT);    // We will control our lander's lights as an OUTPUT.
}

/*
 * After the setup function, the loop function starts. This function keeps repeating over and over, like
 * a circle. Here, we are making our light turn on and off in a repeating pattern.
 */
void loop() {
  digitalWrite(CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN, HIGH);  // This line turns the lander's light ON.
  delay(1000);                           // Wait for one second (1000 milliseconds) with the light ON.
  digitalWrite(CABIN_LIGHTS_PIN, LOW);   // This line turns the lander's light OFF.
  delay(100);                            // Wait for a tenth of a second (100 milliseconds) with the light OFF.
}

Astrid:
"Wakey wakey, Explorer. The dawn of Day 3 is upon us and, as much as I'd love to let you slumber in peace, we have an ocean's depth of work to tackle.

Do you recall our incessantly blinking LED from yesterday? Well, today we're going to show those painstaking glimmers of light who's boss. Enter the dip switch. This nifty little piece of hardware will allow us to control that blinking at our will. It's going to be the relentless leash for our overzealous LED.

Of course, to make this happen, we're going to need more than just some physical wiring. This is where things get cerebral. We're going to be introducing some conditional logic into our code - a touch of 'if this, then that'.

Alright then, a flashy switch and some smart code are on our horizon. Rest assured, by the end of the day, we'll have our blinking light under control. So, whenever you’re ready Explorer! …Though, regardless of your readiness, we must begin!"

"Right, let's not dilly-dally. Our main character today is the dip switch. This fellow might look simple with its array of tiny switches, but oh, can it wield a great deal of power.

You see, 'dip' stands for 'dual in-line package'. Quite the mouthful, isn't it? It's essentially a manual electric switch that's packaged with others in a group. The switches are all housed in a single box and can be toggled on or off independently. You may think of it like a tiny panel of light switches, each controlling a separate room in a miniature dollhouse.

Each position of the individual switches in a dip switch represents a binary condition, which simply means either ON or OFF, HIGH or LOW, one or zero, yes or no. The magic lies in the fact that you can manipulate these switches to control different parts of a circuit.

In our case, the part of the circuit we're so keen to dominate is that perpetually blinking LED. By the end of today, that LED will be blinking only when we want it to. The power of control is within our grasp, Explorer. So let's grab it."

[somewhere we need an explanation for what ground and vcc/5V/power and why they are needed to complete a circuit, also maybe what a complete circuit even is. Not sure if best here or elsewhere] (Unfamiliar with these elements - would need some background info)

"Ah, the extra resistor! A keen observer, aren't you, Explorer? You see, the extra resistor we're talking about is what we call a 'pull-down' resistor. Its job is to make sure our circuit behaves properly when the switch is OFF.

Without it, our switch being in the OFF position could cause our circuit to get all… wonky. Imagine it like a crowd of electricity not knowing where to go and causing a ruckus. We certainly don't need more chaos around here.

What the pull-down resistor does is finish the circuit and acts like a form of crowd control. When the switch is OFF, it guides the confused electricity safely away, down to the ground. This way, our circuit stays composed and organized, just the way we like it.

Remember, Explorer, every brick in a building plays a vital role in keeping it standing. Similarly, every small component in a system is crucial to its overall functionality and success. Now, let's return to our task at hand."

An input is essentially a message received by our Hero board. It could be from a sensor, a button, or in our case today, a dip switch. These inputs tell the board what's happening out in the world - in this instance, whether our dip switch is ON or OFF (HIGH or LOW).

On the other hand, outputs are [minor removal occurred here] signals sent out by the Hero board. Outputs could be triggered by code conditions or other sensor readings... like the position of a switch. Examples include spinning a motor, sounding a buzzer, or making an LED blink or stay solid.

Astrid:
"Alright, time for a bit of theory. Fear not, Explorer, it won't bite. 

Conditional logic involves creating rules within your code based on certain conditions. much like deciding to wear a spacesuit before you step out into the vacuum of space. Simple, would you agree?

The main tools for this in [minor removal occurred here] C plus plus (C++) language are 'if', 'else if', and 'else' statements. Let's break them down:

- 'if': This is the primary decision-making tool in your code. If a certain condition is true, then some code will run. For example, `if (oxygen level is less than critical)`, we initiate emergency procedures. This only happens if the condition is true. If the oxygen level is measured to be fine, the code within the if statement is ignored.

- 'else if': This comes into play when you have more than one condition to check. It’s like saying, 'if the first condition isn’t true and doesn’t apply, we’ll evaluate the next one.' It’s important to note that an 'else if' always follows an 'if' or another 'else if'.

- 'else': This is the last resort. If none of the previous 'if' or 'else if' conditions are true, then the code in the 'else' block will run. It's like saying, 'if nothing else works out, let's do this.'

Here’s a quick example:




You’ll notice that we always indent everything inside sets of { } curly brackets. This simply allows for better readability of the code. 





In this code, the ship checks the oxygen level and responds appropriately based on the conditions we've set.

I hope this brings some light into the murky depths of conditional logic for you. Let's see if we can apply this to our switch situation next!"

When the switch is ON, [minor removal and rewording occurred here] 'digital Write()' is executed, turning the LED on or HIGH as you could say. This sends current to the LED.

If the switch is OFF, the 'else' block is executed instead. Here, 'digital Write()' is run, which grounds the LEDs circuit, turning it off.

So, this code is continuously checking the state of your switch and turning the LED on or off accordingly. Quite easy, don’t you think? Now let's try this out on your end, Explorer."

Oh? My, I’ll give you credit, you have a keen eye. Good on you for noticing that. You've stumbled upon one of the classic conundrums of programming, Explorer. The tale of the two equals. They might look similar, but they have different roles in the programming universe. Let's clear up the confusion, shall we?

In programming, a single equals sign (=) is known as the 'assignment operator'. It's used when you want to assign a value to a variable. Think of it as the word 'is' in English. For example, int my variable equals ten semicolon ('int myVariable = 10;') is like saying, my variable is ten ('myVariable is 10').

Now, a double equals sign (==) is a completely different beast. This is the 'equality operator', and it's used to compare two values to see if they're equal. If they are, the comparison returns 'true'. If they're not, it returns 'false'. This is used a lot in conditional statements, like 'if' statements.

Here's an example:






So remember, one equals sign(=) is for assignment, and a double equals sign(==) is for comparison. It's crucial not to mix these up. Trust me, it will save you a lot of headache in the future."

Astrid:

"Ah, welcome, my intrepid Explorer, to the grand finale of week one or, as some might call it, day four. I must confess, you've performed surprisingly well. For a human, that is.

First things first! The hardware. Notice how we've added a couple of LEDs and some extra wires for our dip switch. Don't start flipping switches just yet though. Without proper instructions in your code, they'll just stare back at you with the cold, lifeless gaze of purposeless tech.

And that brings us to our code, our all-important guide to technological obedience. We're defining six new variables that will tell us which pins are pulling their weight in the circuit. In the setup() function, we'll use the pin Mode() function to designate switches as INPUTs and LEDs as OUTPUTs. Finally, we'll march into the loop() function and create three if statements - our conditional knights in shining armor. They'll relay the status of the three dip switches.

So, my brave Explorer, are you ready to turn our ship bright enough to lure all manner of prey and predators in this unexplored ocean, like squidboats on Earth? I know - I'm excited too! Once you’ve finished wiring, upload your new code to the HERO and let there be light!"

You know the drill! Let’s cut to some instructional material I was able to gather cached on the computer. And you guessed it, I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. I’ll see you soon!”

Astrid Script:
"Good morning, Explorer. Or is it evening? Oh, who knows under this ocean blanket. Now, let’s wipe that taxed look off your face and - oh, that IS your face? Oops.Get ready to tackle our next big task. We can't ignore this any longer. Our battery levels are going critical. Our Hero experiments and shipboard lighting barely draw anything, but what's killing us is just keeping things warm. I've had to shut down sections C-to-F, so... no more jelly parkour, I'm afraid."

Our solar array, which normally bathes in glorious sunlight to charge our batteries, is as inactive as a frozen comet. It’s imperative that we restore it! Luckily, our kit is equipped with materials that have been waiting oh-so-patiently to be introduced - photoresistors! 

For our training purposes, it's enough to know that photoresistors also react to light like solar panels, but in a different way. Photoresistors react to sunlight like summer flowers - When the sun rises, flowers open. When the sun goes down, flowers close. With photoresistors, when they receive light, their resistance decreases, allowing more current to pass, or higher signal levels as read. In darkness, resistance increases, choking current, lowering signal levels. We can use this to simulate our solar panels receiving sunlight. 

So, brace yourself, dear Explorer. Day six (6) begins, and we have a solar system to fix."
.
.
"Let’s get to know our newest shining star, shall we? This little device is also known as a light-dependent resistor or L-D-R for short. The resistance of a photoresistor decreases with increasing incident light intensity. In simpler terms, when there's more light, the flow of electrons becomes easier; when there’s less light, the flow becomes harder. Think of it as an electrical component throwing a 'more light, more fun' party.

Photoresistors are made of high-resistance semiconductor material. Whew! Try saying that five times fast. When light hits the device, it absorbs the light photons. This absorption process provides energy to the electrons, which makes them jump from their 'valence band' of orbits to the 'conduction band.' (What is a valence band and a conduction band?) This leap means the electrons are free to move, thus reducing the material's resistance. It's like magic, only its scientific nature is backed up by empirical evidence and testable explanations.

Essentially, a photoresistor is a sensor that allows us to detect light in the way that a solar panel uses it to generate power. They are the key component we need to simulate and fix our solar array today.

Remember, they are small and sensitive. Treat them well, and they'll return the favor in kind, Explorer!"

"Moving right along… Let's delve into a slightly more complex topic - the concept of analog and digital signals. Do bear with me, Explorer, as this will be vital for us to understand our photoresistor readings.

Alright. Analog signals! These are continuous signals. Imagine a dimmer switch connected to a light bulb. The dimmer can be adjusted to any level between on and off, providing an infinite number of positions of brightness. This is like an analog signal where the values can fall anywhere within a certain range. 

In terms of microcontrollers, they are a bit more complex to handle due to their nature of being prone to degradation and noise interference. What this complexity does, however, is allow them to represent numerous amounts of information with the potential to provide more accurate and refined data.

Unlike analog signals, digital signals are discrete and can only be in one of two states, which are usually represented by either zeros (0’s) or ones (1’s) (LOW and HIGH). Picture a staircase. Each step you take is a definite value. There are no in-betweens! While they might not be as 'smooth' or 'precise' as their analog counterparts, digital signals are highly resistant to noise and easy to work with in terms of programming. Most of our microcontroller functions, like digitalWrite and digitalRead, work with digital signals.

So, where do our photoresistors fit in? I’m glad you asked! The value they provide, based on light intensity, is an analog signal - it can theoretically be an infinite number of values. That's why, to read these values, we need to use a different set of functions that can handle this variety - the analog functions. But more on that when we dive into the code.

In summary, remember: Analog is like a dimmer - continuous and smooth while digital is like stairs - definite and distinct. Got it? Great!"
.
.
"Alright, Explorer, I know you've been wondering why of all the many pins, we specifically chose the A-Zero (A0) pin on the Hero board for our photoresistor? Well, let me break it down for you.

You see, A-Zero (A0) is an analog pin. Now, our photoresistor isn't just a simple 'light or no light' type of device. No, it's much more refined than that. It measures 'how MUCH light there is.' It's like the difference between asking if there's food at a party and asking how much food is there. I mean, a few crumbs of chips don't really count, do they?

Anyway, because we want a wide range of values and not just a zero (0) or one (1), we need an analog pin, hence A-Zero (A0). It's really as simple as that - analog pins for analog devices, digital pins for digital devices."

"Well done, Explorer, you've digested all that analog and digital chit-chat pretty well. Now, let's shift our attention to the programming part of our task. And what-do-ya know? We've got another new tool in our kit to introduce - the Arduino Serial Monitor.

The Serial Monitor is one of the most useful tools we have at our disposal when programming with the Arduino I-D-E (IDE). Think of it as a window, through which your computer can have a conversation with the microcontroller. In this case, our Hero board.

With the Serial Monitor, we can send data to the Hero board and receive data from it. It can also be used to debug (the act of finding and removing bugs in our code), examine the state of variables, or, like we'll be doing today, monitor the readings from our photoresistors.

To access it, we'll need to start by including some code in our sketch to establish a Serial communication. Then, when we want to take a peek at the data being sent from the Hero board to the computer, we simply click on the 'Serial Monitor' option in the 'Tools' menu in the Arduino I-D-E (IDE).

Ready to give it a shot, Explorer? Let's dive into the code and start using the Serial Monitor to get a sense of what our photoresistor is telling us about our power situation."

"We're going to start using some new commands today, starting with 'analogRead()'. This function allows our Hero board to read analog input signals, which is just what we need for our photoresistors.

AnalogRead() measures the voltage between zero (0), which represents zero-V (0V), and one-thousand-twenty-three (1023) which represents five-V (5V), and returns a corresponding value. In essence, when we use 'analogRead()', we're asking the Hero board to take a voltage reading from a specific analog pin and translate it into a number we can use in our code.

'Reading' and 'writing' are two fundamental operations in HERO programming, or any programming for that matter! When we 'read', we're taking data or information in. When we 'write', we're sending data or information out.

In Arduino terms, 'reading' is often associated with sensors or inputs, like our determined photoresistors. They 'read' or gather information from the physical world which is used to make decisions in our code. On the other hand, 'writing' is generally associated with actuators or outputs, like L-E-D’s (LEDs) or motors. We 'write' to them to control their behavior based on the logic in our code.

Alright, Explorer, are you ready to put this into practice? Let's start with 'analogRead()' and see what we can learn from our photoresistors!"

It’s about that time! Let’s cut to some instructional material that I was able to gather, cached on the computer. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. Hurry on, now!

The photoresistor is successfully reading light levels, meaning our battery is now charging, thus improving our power and oxygen situation exponentially. It's impressive what a motivated human can accomplish when faced with a little adversity!

Don't get too comfortable, however. Just because you've bought us some more time, doesn't mean you can take a break just yet. While it's nice to breathe without worry, remember, we're still sitting under an ocean with a multitude of broken systems on our hands. Getting our spaceship back to the surface and launching back into orbit is still a herculean task.

Still, today's success should bring some optimism. Tomorrow, we'll gather some better analytics of our charging rate by adding some additional code to our sketch. For now, power down, and rest up. You've earned it, Explorer. Until the next sunrise, that is. Bye for now!
Arduino/HERO Code (.ino):
/*
 * 30 Days - Lost in Space
 * Day 6 - Time to Fix the Battery
 *
 * Learn more at https://learn.inventr.io/adventure
 *
 * We will need to find an additional source of power as our battery will not last long
 * without some recharging.  We do have solar panels so let's work on how the HERO can
 * monitor the power coming from those panels using a "photo-resistor" to simulate the
 * variable power available to our Lander.
 *
 * Alex Eschenauer
 * David Schmidt
 * Greg Lyzenga
 */

/*
 * Arduino concepts introduced/documented in this lesson.
 * - unsigned int: A 16 bit value containing numbers from 0 to 65535
 * - Serial Monitor: Allows the HERO to display text in an Arduino IDE window.
 * - Serial.begin(): Used to initialize the Serial Monitor.
 * - Serial.print(): Display some text in the Arduino IDE Serial Monitor window.
 * - Serial.println(): Display test in the Serial Monitor followed by a newline.
 * - analogRead(): Read a value from an analog pin that is based on how much voltage is on the pin (0-5v)
 *
 * Parts and electronics concepts introduced in this lesson.
 * - Analog pins: Pins on the HERO that can read many different values instead of just HIGH/LOW.
 * - Photo Resistor: Changes it's resistance depending on how much light it senses.
 */

/* We start by including this line of code, which helps our HERO work properly with the Arduino program. */
#include "Arduino.h"

// Our photoresistor will give us a reading of the current light level on this analog pin
const byte PHOTORESISTOR_PIN = A0;  // we pick an analog pin (defined in Arduino.h)

// These two constants set the minimum and maximum delay times for oue blinking LED
// The type "unsigned int" represents numbers from 0 to 65535.  Another name you may
// see for this type is "uint16_t".  We need this since these delay values can be
// greater than the biggest number a byte can represent (255).
const unsigned int MIN_DELAY = 50;   // 50 ms shortest blink delay
const unsigned int MAX_DELAY = 500;  // 500 ms longest blink delay

// One time setup
void setup() {
  // We will blink our build in LED based on amount of light received from our photoresistor
  pinMode(LED_BUILTIN, OUTPUT);       // output since we will control the built in LED
  pinMode(PHOTORESISTOR_PIN, INPUT);  // input value from analog pin connected to photoresistor

  /*
   * To show you the exact value being read on the analog pin we will print the exact number
   * using our Arduino IDE's "Serial Monitor".  This is a window displayed under the sketch that
   * can display text sent to it from the HERO.
   *
   * The speed that this data is sent/received must match between the Arduino IDE and HERO.  We
   * configure this speed for the HERO to send data using the Serial.begin() function.  Throughout
   * this course we will use a typical value of 9600 "baud", which is 9,600 bit of information per
   * second.
   */
  Serial.begin(9600);  // This initializes the Serial Monitor and sets the speed to 9600 bits per second
}

// The loop() function is called over and over when sketch is run.
void loop() {
  /*
   * Each time through our loop() we will read the current value of our photoresistor.  When
   * the voltage goes up from more light we flash the built-in LED faster (with shorter blinks).
   *
   * The photoresistor will produce a voltage that is not a simple digitial (on/off, HIGH/LOW)
   * value.  Because of this we cannot use a digital pin on our HERO.  Luckily our HERO board
   * provides six "analog" pins, named A0-A5.
   *
   * These pins will convert a voltage from 0V to 5V to a number from 0 to 1023, giving us
   * a full range of values.  Because we are reading an analog value instead of a digital
   * value, we use the analogRead() function.
   *
   * Here we use the reading from the PHOTORESISTOR_PIN and modify how long we delay based on it.
   */
  unsigned int light_value = analogRead(PHOTORESISTOR_PIN);   // light value from 0 to 1024

  /*
   * Our HERO board doesn't have a text display, so the Arduino IDE has provided us a way to
   * for the HERO to send messages that are displayed in a window in the Arduino IDE.  To open
   * the Serial Monitor on the Arduino IDE you can select Tools/Serial Monitor from the Arduino
   * IDE menu, use the shortcut keys listed in the menu or click the "magnifying glass" icon in
   * the upper right of the IDE window.
   *
   * We initialized the Serial functions in setup and now we will use Serial.print() and
   * Serial.println() to send messages to the Arduino IDE Serial Monitor.  Both functions
   * take a single value which can be either a constant or a variable.  The only difference
   * between the two functions is that Serial.print() leaves the cursor at the end of the
   * current line.  Any additional output will be appended to the current line.  Serial.println()
   * prints the value and THEN adds a "newline", so that any following messages will appear
   * on the following line.
   */
  Serial.print("Light value: ");  // Display label string to serial monitor
  Serial.print(light_value);      // display the value read from our photoresistor

  /*
   * The flash rate varies based on the relative brightness received by the photoresistor.
   * However, your brightness levels could be very different if you are in a dark room from
   * running this in a bright sunny room.  Because of this we will save the darkest value
   * and the brightest value seen.
   *
   * These values would normally be lost when each run of our loop() ends, but by adding
   * the "static" declaration we indicate that these local variables should maintain their
   * values between loop() runs.
   *
   * The FIRST time these variables are used they will get initialized with the first light_value
   * but that initialization is only done the first time so they will retain any changes
   * between the loop() runs.
   */
  static unsigned int darkest_light = light_value;    // this is the lowest value returned by the photoresistor
  static unsigned int brightest_light = light_value;  // this is the highest value returned by the photoresistor

  /*
   * Now that we have a light value let's update our darkest and brightest values
   * if the current value is darker (less than) our previous darkest or brighter
   * (greater than) our previous brightest value.
   */
  if (light_value < darkest_light) {  // If value is LESS THAN ('<') the darkest...
    darkest_light = light_value;      // ...then save current value as our new darkest value
  }
  if (light_value > brightest_light) {  // If value is GREATER THAN ('>') the brightest...
    brightest_light = light_value;      // ...then save current value as our new brightest value
  }

  /*
   * Now we have an interesting problem.  As our voltage reading goes up, we want the LED to
   * blink faster (and thus with a smaller delay).  In addition, we need to adjust the blink
   * rate in a noticeable way.  So, we have one range of numbers (darkest to brightest values)
   * from our light sensor that we wish to convert to a different range that goes from MAX_DELAY
   * down to MIN_DELAY.
   *
   * The Arduino language provides a neat function named map() that was created just for this
   * situation.  It takes a value from one range and *maps* it into a desired range.  It properly
   * handles the situation where the second range goes DOWN when the first range goes UP.
   *
   * We introduce it's used here to map our light_value (which goes from darkest_light to
   * brightest_light) and return a value from MAX_DELAY *down* to MIN_DELAY.  Perfect!
   */
  unsigned int delay_value = map(light_value, darkest_light, brightest_light, MAX_DELAY, MIN_DELAY);
  Serial.print(", Delay value: ");  // display label after light_value
  Serial.println(delay_value);      // display delay_value returned by map() function with newline

  // now blink our built in LED using our delay_value.
  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);  // Turn on LED
  delay(delay_value);               // Leave on delay_value milliseconds
  digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW);   // Turn off LED
  delay(delay_value);               // Leave off delay_value milliseconds
}

Astrid Script:
"Rise and shine, explorer! Our oxygen levels are steady, and our battery is replenishing nicely. That's cause for a small sigh of relief, wouldn't you say? But let's not lose sight of the task at hand; we have another glorious day of repairs to conduct.

Today, we're going to shift gears and delve into a bit of digital logic. We'll be learning about bits, data types, and why choosing the right one for your variables can make all the difference. Efficient memory usage is essential for our system to function optimally, especially on low-memory devices like our Hero board.

Moreover, we're going to expand the depth of our coding efforts. We'll work on a piece of code whose intention is to calculate our ship's battery percentage. Not only will this give us a clearer idea of our power situation, but it will also be a practical application of the concepts we're learning. Ready to get your hands dirty with some binary? Of course you are! Let's get to it."

"Well, well, aren't you lucky, Explorer? Today you get to enjoy a bit of a hardware holiday. How delightful for you! We're leaving the wires and boards be – they've had quite enough of your 'expertise' for the time being.

Before you start daydreaming about a leisurely day off, let me assure you, we're not on vacation. There is still much work to be done! While the hardware is blessedly out of harm's way for a day, we're going to fill the void with a thrilling deep-dive into the captivating world of coding. Yes, that's right – more mesmerizing mazes of bits, bytes, variables, and functions are waiting for your full attention.

This isn't just about expanding your tiny knowledge base for the sake of it, you know. We're doing this so we can craft more refined, more efficient code. In layman's terms: smoother operations and quicker fixes lead us to a faster escape from this underwater blunder that we’re in.

So, buckle up, Explorer. It's high time you familiarize yourself with the finer points of our Hero board's language. We've got some serious coding to do."
.
.
.
"Oh, joy. It seems you're thirsty for knowledge, Explorer. Well, hold on to your seat; I'm about to drop some wisdom bombs on you. 

First, there's this delightful little thing called a 'bit.' The smallest unit of data there is! It’s binary, which in this case means it’s either zero (0) or one (1). Its simplicity is quite adorable, isn’t it? Combine three of these and boom! You have eight unique integer numbers. Mathemagic!

Next, let's graduate to the 'integer.' It's a little bit chunkier, with a typical size of sixteen (16) bits. This gives you a marvelous range of about plus or minus thirty-two thousand, seven-hundred-sixty-eight (32,768). Not too shabby, wouldn’t you agree?

When it comes to integers, we’re not bound by one path to stroll down. We've got two! 'Signed' and 'unsigned.' The signed integers take more of a straightforward path, and have a bit that acts like a flag for whether the number is positive or negative. Unsigned integers, on the other hand, don't bother with this. As free spirits, they take the scenic route, living their life positively all the way! Hence the bigger range of numbers.

Now, take our photoresistor. It's a modest creature, outputting a value between zero and one-thousand-twenty-three (0-1023). It's just ten (10) bits worth of data because two to the power of ten (2^10) equals one-thousand-twenty-four (1024). So, an integer is more than adequate to store it. But don't get too comfortable. Our batteryCapacity variable is set at a lofty fifty-thousand (50,000), which outclasses the standard short integer maximum of thirty-two-thousand, seven-hundred-sixty-eight (32,768). Thus, it needs the V-I-P (VIP) treatment which defines it as an unsigned integer.

Next up, meet 'float.' This one likes to live a bit on the edge, handling decimal numbers with precision. Imagine one-hundred-twenty-three point four-five-six-seven (123.4567) – it has a precision of seven (7). Not bad, eh?

Then there's 'double.' It's a float that hit the gym and got buff. It's sixty-four (64) bits of power, capable of holding values up to a mind-boggling ten to the power of three-hundred-eight (10^308).

Veering off from variables, let's shine some light on 'void.' It's what we use when we're defining functions that don't give anything back. It’s a bit of an altruist. But don’t worry! We’ll dive deeper into creating our own function soon enough.”

So there you have it. A crash course in basic digital logic. Its overwhelming nature is similar to drinking from a firehose, I know. But fret not, we'll be practicing these concepts soon enough. Shall we push on then?"
.
Today’s mission involves writing additional logic in order to print the battery percentage to our handy serial monitor that we discussed yesterday. 

Of course, we don’t want to spend time rewriting the print statements all over the place in our code. Instead, we can create a new function. 

We can start with the word “void”. This is what the function will be returning. As you may have guessed, “void” means that it will return nothing. This also means that we don’t need a return statement, since the compiler deems it unnecessary. I recommend you name it something relative, such as “PrintBatteryPercentage().” The parentheses can be empty since we have no parameters we need to pass into the function. 

Finally, everything inside the brackets is what will execute when you call the function. When the second closing bracket is reached, it will return to where it was called from in the code. If my assumption is correct, it is likely ‘setup’ or ‘function’. 

In the end, your newly created function might look something like this:

For the line-by-line, let's jump to the information cached on your computer. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. I’ll be waiting patiently for your return.

[segway to inventrkits line by line]

"Well, well, well, you've made it through the day, Explorer. Our little adventure with bits, integers, floats, and doubles didn't scare you off, did it? Not bad for a human, I must say.

Now that we have our power situation under control and can monitor our battery percentage, you must be feeling rather accomplished! Before you get lost in celebration, let me remind you that we've barely scratched the surface of what's broken on this ship.

Tomorrow, we're going to brighten up this dreary spacecraft by adding a bit of color. Yes, you heard me right. We're going to introduce an R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED), which can emit all the colors of the rainbow. We'll use this flashy little gizmo for various status indicators - like painting a glowing green if our battery is all charged up, or a glaring red if we're running on fumes.

So, get some rest, Explorer. Tomorrow, we paint with light."

Astrid Script:
"Rise and shine, Explorer! Day eight (8) has dawned upon us and has left us with no time to waste! Today, we're stepping up our game. We're going to add a dash of color to your monotonous existence by using a nifty piece of hardware called an R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED). Allow me to explain.  

RGB LED stands for Red-Green-Blue Light Emitting Diode. What this L-E-D (LED) does is allow us to produce virtually any color on the spectrum by controlling the intensity of the three colored diodes. It’s like your own personal rainbow producer! 

We're also going to cover a new function today: analogWrite(). It allows us to send an analog output signal on a digital pin. Yes, I know what you're thinking: didn’t we just discuss how digital and analog are separate entities? We sure did! Let me, however, introduce you to the world of 'Pulse Width Modulation' or P-W-M (PWM). This savvy, little trick allows us to fake an analog output on a digital pin! Pretty neat, wouldn’t you agree?

Nonetheless, that’s enough jabber for now! Rest assured, all this and more will be covered as the day progresses. Brace yourself in, Explorer. We've got colors to mix and a spaceship to fix!"
.
.
"An R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) is unlike your usual kind of LED, Explorer. It is like a three-in-one special! Inside that tiny package, you'll find three separate L-E-Ds (LEDs): one Red, one Green, and one Blue, as these are the primary colors of light.

In a regular L-E-D (LED), you apply power and it emits a pre-programmed, specific color. In an R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED), however, you have the ability to control each of the three colors individually. You can turn on the red L-E-D (LED), the green L-E-D (LED), the blue L-E-D (LED) - or any combination of the three!

So, why is this exciting? Because by varying the intensity of each of these colors, you have the power to create virtually any color you want right at your fingertips! How deeply thrilling! 

Need a soothing aquamarine glow to relax? Easy. Want to flash a warning in bright, alarming red? Ask and you shall receive!

In essence, with an R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED), you're not just getting one color. You're getting the whole spectrum. Enjoy the possibilities, Explorer!"
.
.
"Okay, Explorer, let's dig into two intertwined concepts: 'analogWrite()' and 'Pulse Width Modulation' or P-W-M (PWM), which are critical for controlling our  R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED).

'AnalogWrite()' is a function in Arduino that helps us generate P-W-M (PWM) signals. Despite its name, oddly enough, 'analogWrite()' doesn't really deal with analog signals. It is a bit of a misnomer, which could be a source of confusion. Have no fear, Explorer! It’ll all piece together soon enough.

Moving onto P-W-M (PWM)! ‘Pulse Width Modulation’ is a technique used to create a kind of 'fake' analog effect using digital means. By rapidly switching a signal or power source on and off, and changing the proportion of the time it is on versus the time it is off, we can vary the intensity of power that is received. This is important for our R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) because it allows us to control the intensity of each of the red, green, and blue LEDs, which in turn lets us mix our colors.

Imagine P-W-M (PWM) as a fast-flickering light. If it flickers so fast that your eyes can't catch it turning on and off, the light will appear dimmer or brighter based on the ratio of 'on' time versus 'off' time. If it's on more than it's off, it appears brighter; if it's off more than it's on, it appears dimmer.

In terms of our R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED), we'll use 'analogWrite()' to send P-W-M (PWM) signals to each of the red, green, and blue L-E-Ds (LEDs). By changing the duty cycle of the P-W-M (PWM) signal — that's the 'on' time — we can control the brightness of each L-E-D (LED). By mixing different levels of brightness across the red, green, and blue L-E-Ds (LEDs), we'll create the color we want.

You’re in for quite a treat, Explorer! The trajectory of your future will soon become a lot brighter…quite literally. 
.
.
"It appears we’ve ‘talked the talk,’ now let’s ‘walk the walk’ Explorer. Our task today is to light up our R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) and give it a whirl! Remember, with Pulse Width Modulation, we can tinker with the intensity of each color, from a meager zero (0) to a dazzling two-hundred-fifty-five (255). Talk about a spectrum!

Now, one way to control our R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) would be to separately define the red, green, and blue values every single time we want a color change. But, frankly, that's like choosing to walk across the galaxy when you have a perfectly good spaceship. It's slow, and it'll leave your code looking like a debris field of a supernova.

Let's be smarter, shall we? What do you say we build a function to streamline the process? Something catchy and memorable like ‘set R-G-B red level, green level, blue level’ (SetRGB(red_level, green_level, blue_level)). You'll see those three “byte” variables nestled inside the parentheses, separated by commas. These are parameters, and we'll be handing them over to our function like instructions to a well-trained robot butler. We don't have a lot of memory, so since the max value doesn’t need to be anything higher than 255 anyway (remember pulse width modulation), a byte is perfect since it’s max value just so happens to be 255. Using something like an integer for an unsigned number <=255 is just a waste of memory. 

Once we're inside the function, it's time to put those parameters to work. We'll use 'analogWrite()' to set the value for each of our three color pins. Efficient and tidy, just the way I like it.


In our main loop, it's testing time. We'll call our snazzy new function in a variety of ways to make sure our R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) isn't just sitting there like a lump of plastic. Once we're confident everything's shipshape, we'll beam it up to our board and enjoy the show.

Let's get to it, Explorer. Lights, camera, code!"

Now for what you’ve been waiting oh-so patiently for. Let’s cut to some instructional material that I was able to gather, cached on the computer. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. See you soon! 

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]

"Well, Explorer, I must say I'm impressed. You've wired up an R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) and successfully programmed it to show different colors based on given values.

Your code is no longer a maze of repeated color values. It's streamlined and efficient like the inner workings of a cutting-edge spaceship. And our Hero Board is sparkling with colors. Just the kind of bright glimmer of hope you need in the depths of an underwater world.

Now, don't get too cozy in your success. Tomorrow, we'll take another momentous leap forward. I see a dashboard display in our future! It's time to start getting some visual feedback from our various ship systems on a display. Yes, indeed. Just because we're marooned in an alien sea doesn't mean we can't have a bit of style, right?

So rest up, Explorer. We have many more lines of code to write and much more hardware to wrangle before our ship can once again break free from this watery prison. But for now, bask in the glory of your multicolored triumph. Tomorrow awaits!"

Astrid Script:
"Greetings, Explorer! Day 9 is upon us, and it's time you stopped ignoring me!
... Or at least, stopped ignoring our battery levels. Getting a percentage is fine, but ultimately, reading that percentage is on you, and you're not always looking. You will have to look after yourself in the future - I'm not going to be around forever, Explorer... I have a spin class at 5...
Let's set up a RGB LED 'rag' status light. Wow! I think that's the most TLAs I've said in a sentence!
... Uh, you know what LED stands for. 'RGB' stands for Red Green Blue, and when you combine these three colors in different intensities, you get all the colors of the rainbow. 'Rag' is another TLA, or three-letter-acronym, and that stands for Red, Amber, Green, otherwise known as a traffic light system.
So... Our mission is to use one LED, which makes any color, to tell you if the battery levels are good-and-green, anxious-amber, or rock-bottom red. RGB LED for RAG ASAP!
Now there are a LOT of decisions to be made on this mission, Explorer. Fortunately, we don't have to make any of them. We'll bring THESE new ways to compare things [graphic of comparison ops] and we'll use a new command, 'else if' to catch any crazy stuff in the code. 'else if' is another conditional operator. .
.
"Right then! Since we're not adding any new hardware today, we'll dive straight into the code pool. Our mission? To create a color-coded battery status indicator using our trusty R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED).

Imagine this, Explorer: A bold green shine to signify a robust battery level. A shifting amber for when power starts to wane. And an urgent red pulsating from our little R-G-B L-E-D (RGB LED) as a warning when the battery drops too low. My mind's eye could wander forever! Let’s make it happen. 
.
.
"Remember when we used the photoresistor to regulate our charging speed?"


Now, we're taking it up a notch. Every loop cycle, we'll work out the battery percentage. Then, we'll test that percentage: if it's within a certain zone, we'll make the light a certain color.

The second step is to use conditional logic to give our ship a color-coded mood ring. Let's see...

Green: brimming with power. Fifty (50) to One-hundred-percent (100%) and raring to go.
Amber: Keep an eye on it, as we're between twenty-five-percent (25%) to fifty-percent (50%).
Red: Danger zone! We're below twenty-five-percent (25%) and need to seriously consider charging up.

You're smart, Explorer. Our RGB LED only has Red, Green and Blue colors - Where's the Yellow??? Well, we can get yellow by mixing a little green with a little red [graphic]"

A true artisan of code you are, Explorer. Now, let's get this show on the road!"
.
.
"In order to make our color-coded power level dreams a reality, it looks like we're going to need a few more tools in our coding toolkit. It's time to get to know some of the more... sophisticated comparison operators.

Allow me to introduce you to our new friends: greater than or equal to, greater than, less than or equal to, and less than (>=, >, <=, and <). Oh, and let's not forget about their pals, the logical operators: AND (&&) and OR (||).

These operators allow us to make more complex decisions in our code.

To start, the greater than (>) and less than (<) signs are likely familiar to you. These symbols are used to compare two values. For example, five is greater than three (5 > 3) returns true because five (5) is indeed greater than three (3).

The less than or equal to (<=) and greater than or equal to (>=) operators extend this comparison. How about this - Is 5 greater than... 5?... We might need a condition where a value is both greater than AND equal to another, and that's why THESE chicken footprints (graphic, <=, >=) are very useful.

I knew you’d catch on fast. Now, onto the logical operators!

'AND' (&&) is a binary operator that requires two conditions to be true for the overall expression to be true. Stay with me now, Explorer. Let’s break it down. Imagine this: you're planning to leave your cozy spacecraft to explore the boundless wonder that is space. Extravehicular Activity, or E-V-A (EVA) as we like to call it. You would only go if both your suit was functional AND the exterior conditions were safe. Clearly, only when both of these conditions are met would you step out of the spacecraft.

'OR' (||) is slightly different. In this case, if either condition is true, then the overall expression is true. Picture this: you eat if you are hungry OR you eat if it is mealtime. If either condition is true, it's chow time!

"Greater than or equal to, AND, OR... Decisions, decisions, decisions. It can be a lot to think about. That's why we code, Explorer: so machines can made the tedious decisions for us."

At this time, you may indulge in the highly beneficial instructional material that I have gathered, cached on the computer. As you know, I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. Good luck!

Remember when you were struggling with basic switches? Seems like ages ago now! You humans grow up so fast. You've come quite a long way, fixing power and oxygen, lighting up your life, and now you've even got a handy color-coded battery life indicator. Great job! 

"But we need to start ramping your progress up. This is a spaceship, not a submarine. Our mothership's orbit is beginning to degrade. If we don't get our heads above water soon, we'll need to start thinking long term... and batteries don't last forever..."

Astrid Script:
"Ah, you’re up! Hope you're ready for the dawn of a new day in this damp tin can that we're calling home. Week three (3) already, huh? Seems like just yesterday you were fumbling over the basics. Look at you now: power, light, a snazzy R-G-B (RGB) battery indicator. All pretty impressive I must say!

This week, we're shifting gears! Enough with the basic survival needs, it's time we brought this old ship back to life. Our goal is to fix up that control panel whose appearance looks as if a Martian tried to play tic-tac-toe on it.

To start us off, we've got a new tool to familiarize ourselves with - a four-by-four (4x4) keypad. I know, you must be thrilled. Do try not to let it’s looks intimidate you. That’s sixteen (16) whole buttons of potential inputs at our fingertips!

Alongside our new hardware, we've got a new concept to grasp. Brace yourself, because we're diving into the wonderful world of libraries today. Think of libraries as your ultimate cheat sheet: 'shortcuts that make coding easier.'

So, don your programmer's hat, take a sip of that dehydrated coffee, and let's tackle the day ahead. We've got work to do, Explorer!"
.
.
"Alright, let's chat about our new piece of hardware, the four-by-four (4x4) keypad. [text removed here] Each button, when pressed, closes a circuit and sends a signal that we can detect and utilize. It's similar to a keyboard you might use on a computer, but far less complicated.

These keypads are often used in security systems, door entry systems, and all sorts of devices where manual input is necessary. Today, we'll be using it as a control interface for our spaceship.

The real magic happens when each button, or key, is pressed. A unique combination of two connections is made. One connection from the four ‘row’ pins, and one from the four ‘column’ pins. This allows us to know exactly which button was pressed, without needing a separate input pin for each button. [on-screen graphic showing overlap of row and column on 4x4 grid]

Each of these sixteen (16) buttons can serve as an individual input, providing us with a wealth of potential interaction options. I can see it now - we could set a code to trigger a certain action, or assign different tasks to different keys. The possibilities are vast and are waiting to be explored! This also means we can reduce the amount of input pins needed from sixteen (16) to eight (8), which is important when we have limited pin real-estate on our HERO. 

But, before we dive into all of that, let's get this mighty piece of machinery wired up and running, shall we?"
.
.
“My! That was quite easy. Looks like we can skip the breadboard entirely today”.
.
.
“Speaking of easy, let’s hop on to our next coding concept and explore the power of your new [small removal] partner in crime - libraries.

In coding terms, a library is like a pre-packaged set of code that provides specific functionality. Essentially, They give you a lot of new functions to use and make your life easier. 

Now, let’s talk about the new library you will need to install: the Keypad-H (Keypad.h) library. This is a custom library designed specifically for interfacing with keypads of various sizes, like a four-by-four (4x4) keypad, for example. How convenient! It includes functions for detecting when keys are pressed, which keys are pressed, and even some advanced functionality like ‘multi-key detection’.

By using this Keypad library, we can simplify our code and kick our level of efficiency up a notch. Because, who needs more complications when you're trying to repair a spaceship, right?"
.
.
In order to use our library, we will first need to install it. If my calculations are correct, there should be a link located somewhere, cached on your current computer page to a ‘.zip’ file. Download that to your computer, and then in the Arduino IDE, navigate to Tools, then (>) Manage Libraries, then (>) Add .zip library and select the newly downloaded .zip file. Take all the time you need, preferably as quick as you can! I’ll be here waiting patiently for you when you return. Finished? Wonderful! Let’s continue, Explorer. 

The library allows us to accomplish another new practical task and that is to create a new, unique object - a Keypad. It also adds in functions like getKey() to tell us which key is being pressed. In order for the newly installed library to help us, however, we need to first provide it with some relevant information. 

Here is our list of “to-do’s.” We need to tell it how many rows we have, how many columns we have, the pins that we are using for our column pins, the pins that we are using for our row pins (said column before but I’m assuming you meant rows?), and finally we need to create a keymap for the buttons. Whew! That was quite the mouthful. 

Well, we know we have four (4) rows, four (4) columns, and we can take a look at which pins we are using by simply looking at our wiring diagram. 

Next, we have to make a keymap. We can achieve this by utilizing a pair of new data structures in programming - arrays.

Arduino/HERO Code (.ino):
/*
 * 30 Days - Lost in Space
 * Day 11 - Starting your control panel
 *
 * Learn more at https://learn.inventr.io/adventure
 *
 * Today we introduce you to a 4x4 button Keypad.  This keypad is very similar to
 * what is found on a calculator or even a digital phone.  This keypad uses only 8
 * pins to encode 16 buttons by dividing the keypad into rows and columns.  When
 * a button is pressed it can be identified by finding which row pin and which column
 * pin are pressed.
 *
 * We simplify this process by using a library written for decoding similar keypads.
 *
 * Alex Eschenauer
 * David Schmidt
 * Greg Lyzenga
 */

/*
 * Arduino concepts introduced/documented in this lesson.
 * - libraries: Code provided by others that we can use in our sketches
 * - char type: Represents a single character like 'A', 'd', '4' or '*'.
 * - arrays:    Variables of the same type arranged as a list where each item
 *              can be accessed using an index.  Arrays can be one dimensional
 *              like a list (with one index) or two dimensional like a spreadsheet
 *              table with rows and columns (using row and column indexes).
 *
 * Parts and electronics concepts introduced in this lesson.
 */

// Explicitly include Arduino.h
#include "Arduino.h"

/*
 * Instead of writing all the code necessary to check all 8 pins and calculate
 * which button is pressed we will use a "library" that has already been written
 * for us and simply call functions in that library.
 *
 * Please refer to the lesson and lesson videos to load the Keypad library into
 * your Arduino IDE before you use it for the first time.
 *
 * In order to use the Keypad library we include the file "Keypad.h".
 */
#include <Keypad.h>

// Our keypad has 4 rows, each with 4 columns.
const byte ROWS = 4;
const byte COLS = 4;

/*
 * A one dimensional array is like a list of similar variables.  We use an "index" number
 * to access each item.  To access values in an array we use the name of the array followed
 * immediately by the index in square brackets: name[index].  The index can either be a
 * constant ("name[3]") or a variable containing a value:
 *
 * byte my_index = 3;
 * name[my_index] = value;
 *
 * Indexes start with zero, not one.  Think of the index as a number *added* to the first
 * location.  So, to access the first item in our array we add 0.  To access the second
 * item in the array we add 1.
 *
 * In our first array below, ROW_PINS[0] is set to 5 and ROW_PINS[2] is 3.
 */

// These constant arrays contain the pins connected to the keypad's row and column pins.
const byte ROW_PINS[ROWS] = { 5, 4, 3, 2 };
const byte COL_PINS[COLS] = { 6, 7, 8, 9 };

/*
  * Character Type (char)
  *
  * In Arduino C++ we have previousluy used strings of characters using double quotes ("my
  * string").  But for a single character we need to use single quotes to indicate that there
  * is just ONE character ('A').
  */

/*
 * Two dimensional array
 *
 * A two dimensional array combines multiple rows of one-dimensional array into a table
 * using two indexes using square brackets.  To access any item in the array the first
 * index is added to the row, and then the second index is added to the column.
 *
 * In the BUTTONS array below BUTTONS[0][0] is set to the character '1' and BUTTONS[2][3]
 * is set to the character 'C'.
 */

// Since the keypad has 4 rows of 4 columns each we use a two dimensional array to
// configure what character will be returned when each button is pressed.
const char BUTTONS[ROWS][COLS] = {
  { '1', '2', '3', 'A' },  // Row 0
  { '4', '5', '6', 'B' },  // Row 1
  { '7', '8', '9', 'C' },  // Row 2
  { '*', '0', '#', 'D' }   // Row 3
};

/*
 * Using the Keypad.h Library, we are going to define a custom keypad to return
 * the characters we wish when each key is pressed.
 *
 * To do this, we create a heroKeypad object configured with our BUTTONS array,
 * the pins used for the rows and columns and lastly how many rows and how many
 * columns are on our kepad.
 *
 * NOTE: this also does the proper pinMode() commands so we don't need them in our setup()
 */
Keypad heroKeypad = Keypad(makeKeymap(BUTTONS), ROW_PINS, COL_PINS, ROWS, COLS);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);  // Initialize the serial monitor
}

void loop() {
  // heroKeypad.waitForKey() will wait here until any button is pressed and
  // returns the character we defined in our BUTTONS array.
  char pressedButton = heroKeypad.waitForKey();  // Wait until a button is pressed

  // Display the character returned for the button that was pressed.
  Serial.println(pressedButton);
}

"Good morning, explorer. I’ve managed to stop the leak from yesterday, but the alarm system is still totally busted. I trust you've had your morning cup of rocket fuel, because today is going to be a melody of learning. Today, we're going to make some noise. Literally. We're introducing another piece of our cosmic puzzle, the ‘passive buzzer’.

Now don't be fooled by its rather mundane name; this little one can make quite a racket when it wants to. It's a small speaker that can play different frequencies, and we're going to use that ability to our advantage.

Today's mission, should you choose to accept it, is to turn our four by four (4x4) keypad into an eight-bit (8-bit) sounding piano keyboard. That's right, by the end of today, your control panel will have its very own mini synth.  Once we can verify that it’s working, we can re-wire up our alarm system. How's that for turning it up to eleven?

So, buckle in, check your dials, and let's hit the launch button on this day. Sound good? I sure hope so!"
.
.
"Let's talk about our new comrade-in-arms, the ‘passive buzzer’. This small, usually cylindrical component is capable of generating a range of sound frequencies. It's like a tiny speaker that you can program.

In the electrical universe, the passive buzzer is a piezoelectric device. When an oscillating electrical signal is applied to its terminals, it vibrates and creates sound. We control the frequency of this oscillation, which translates to the pitch of the sound that it generates.

To put it simply, you give it a frequency and tell it how long to play it. Simple. To accomplish this, we can use ‘pulse width modulation’, and it sings that note back to you.

So what do you say, Explorer? Let's get ready to compose some galactic symphonies, shall we?"
.
.
Now, in order to make the passive buzzer produce any sound, we need to send it some sort of frequency on to its signal pin. In terms of music, using a different frequency will produce a different sound. It is not possible to generate tones lower than thirty-one hertz (31hz) and struggles to produce higher than four-thousand-nine-hundred-seventy-eight hertz (4978hz). If you want an optional additional library, it may be in your best interest to check out the tone-h (Tone.h) library. That way if you want a specific note, you can say ‘note c-five’ (“NOTE_C5”) instead of some arbitrary number like five-hundred-twenty-three (523). I’ve added a copy to your mission resources. 

Luckily, sending those tones is made easy with a built-in function called “tone()”. Tone() takes two parameters, the pin and the frequency you want to send. Optionally, you can also put a third for duration. 
.
.
"Now, let’s brainstorm together, Explorer. We want each button on our keypad to trigger a specific tone. Oh, I can hear you now... 'Let's just churn out a bunch of if statements, one for each button.' If button one (1) is pressed, play two-hundred hertz (200Hz). If button A is pressed, play six-hundred hertz (600Hz). And so forth. A simplistic approach, but not very elegant, is it?

Instead, let's introduce some finesse into our coding journey. How about we conjure up a second two-dimensional (2D) array? We'll christen it as something suitable, like 'tones'—a title befitting its role. And then, there you have it, we only need ONE if statement.

This method is neater and easier to debug. Plus, it's a great way to flex those hefty programming muscles we've been developing. Let’s continue, shall we?"

I want to take the time to introduce a new programming concept - loops. I’m sure you already know about our loop() function, but the loops we will be talking about today are used in nearly all programming languages universally and are in code everywhere. What I’m referencing are called 'for' and 'while' loops. 

"For loops execute a block of code a number of times, and usually used if you, or the code, knows the number of times it should loop. A For loop looks like this (graphic), and declares the initial loop counter value here, a test of that counter, and by how much the counter changes with each loop."


In this example, 'counter' starts at zero (0), and the loop will continue until 'counter' is no longer less than ten (10). Each time the loop runs, 'i' increases by one (1), which is what ‘i-plus-plus’ ('i++') signifies. Once i is no longer less than 10, the loop is broken and the program moves on to the next line of code.

Next, let's move on to 'while' loops. These are used when we don't know how many times the loop will need to run. It just keeps running as long as a certain condition is true. Once that condition is no longer true, the loop stops. Here's the format:


As long as 'condition' is true, the code inside the curly braces will run. Make sure to alter something within the loop to eventually break the condition, or you'll end up with an endless loop—a space explorer's worst nightmare, believe me.

You get all that? Allow me to give you a snappy rundown then. 'For' loops: you know how many times you need to do a thing. 'While' loops: you don't know how many times you'll do the thing, you just do it until a condition stops being true. You’ve got this, Explorer! I am about ninety-four-point-eight-six-three-percent (94.863%) sure of it.

Fun little trick for you if you want to use it: if you add ‘while(true);’ at the end of setup(), you can halt your code there, to debug the setup() function. You can technically do that wherever in your code to help debug before a specific point.

I suppose I’ve babbled on enough for now. Let’s cut to some instructional material that I was able to gather, cached on the computer. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference.
