Astrid Script:
"A new week dawns, my intrepid explorer, and it brings a fresh set of challenges to our undersea sojourn. You may have been enjoying the soothing serenity of the deep ocean, but it's time to point our sights towards the surface. Now don't get too excited - we can't just jet up there like a dolphin on an energy drink. There are imperative protocols to be followed.

As any experienced Explorer, such as yourself, knows,rising to the surface too quickly will reduce pressure that will cause our spaceship to explode like a shaken soda bottle and I believe neither of us would be particularly fond of that outcome.

First things first: power. We need all we can muster, so it would be best to attempt our surface-bound journey when power generation is at its peak. That means mid-day. To do that, however, we need to know when exactly that is. In other words, we need to build a clock.

There's also the matter of the encrypted source code provided to us. Decrypting it is crucial for all of our plans to unfold. So, we have our work cut out for us this week. We need to create a clock, decrypt the code, and most importantly, limit the rate of ascend to prevent us from becoming a bursting submarine balloon

Now, let's put our crafting cap on and get down to business. We've got a spaceship to guide home."
.
.
"Right then, let's dive into the fascinating world of timekeeping. To craft our clock, we'll be making good use of a charming little component in our kit: the seven-segment (7-segment) display. Intrigued? Well, allow me to explain.

A 7-segment display looks like this. It contains a total of 7 LEDs A-G in an “8” formation.  Each of these LEDs can be individually illuminated to make numbers and other characters.  Simple, yet ingenious.

Let’s Imagine a digit. How about an eight (8)? Notice how it's essentially a formation of different horizontal and vertical segments. Now, think about how you can form other numbers by lighting up or turning off different combinations of these segments. If we turn off the horizontal segment in the center, for example, we turn our eight (8) into a zero (0)! Behold - the power of a seven-segment (7-segment) display. Simple, yet incredibly useful! 


It is not my intention to befuddle you, dear Explorer, but the digits aren't the only things a seven-segment (7-segment) display can render. With a bit of creativity, you can also display a few letters and symbols, but we’ll save those for later.

For now, find security in understanding the basic concept of a seven-segment (7-segment) display and prepare to harness the power of this nifty little device to create our clock. Ready for a bit of digital wizardry, Explorer?"
.
.
"Alright, Explorer, buckle up as we dive into the wiring part of our adventure today. You'll be relieved to know it's quite straightforward with just four wires needed for our seven-segment (7-segment) display module. Here's how we do it:

As always, we connect the ... ground wire here, and positive here. These are our data pins, and need to go here on our digital pins. 

Our friend, C-L-K (CLK), stands for ‘Clock pin’. Its job is to compile the binary data streaming from the D-I-O (DIO) pin into a byte. One cycle equals one byte.

Our other friend, D-I-O (DIO) stands for ‘Digital Input/Output’. Its role is to receive binary data for the HERO board, which in turn, determines which segments of our display are going to be dazzling us with their lights and which ones will remain in the dark.

Think of it like a switchboard operator of the olden days, but for data bits. Now, an eight-bit (8-bit) binary number provides just enough digits to control all these segments, and even gives us a few to spare.

So there you have it - simple, isn't it? The magic of making numbers out of light is within our grasp, so let's press on, shall we?"
.
.
"Now, Explorer, we're going to make a bit of a shift from our hardware chat and delve into the coding realm for the day. Here's where we bring in our secret weapon - ... a brand-new library.






Rest assured, Explorer, I’m happy to provide you with a refresher on library installation. Here’s how it goes. Navigate to the 'Sketch' tab in the top menu, then move to 'Include Library' and click on 'Add dot-Z-I-P (.ZIP) Library'. This way, we'll get all the resources we need without having to invent the wheel all over again. It's quite handy, isn't it?"

Do be sure to add the library to the top of our code so we can utilize it. 

Now, by creating this new object, we’ll be granted access to the entire library of the new seven-segment (7-segment) functions. Remember, you must not forget to define those pins!




The one we’ll be most interested in is the set-segments (setSegments) function. 






This function is like the puppet master behind our seven-segment (7-segment) display.




Allow me to break it down for you. Each of the seven segments of our display, labeled A to G, can be controlled individually and can combine to form different characters. Each segment can either be turned on, meaning it lights up, or off, where it remains dark.

The ‘set-segments’ ('setSegments') function helps us control which segments light up and which do not. It's essentially taking the reins and directing our display to show specific characters.

How does it know what to display, you ask? It takes an array of segment states as an argument. Each bit in the array corresponds to a specific segment. When a bit is ‘one’ ('1'), the corresponding segment lights up. When it's ‘zero’ ('0'), the segment remains off.

In essence, ‘set-segments’ ('setSegments') helps us paint pictures, or rather numbers and letters, on our seven-segment (7-segment) display by controlling the light show for each segment. Pretty cool, isn't it?" 
.
.
"Alright, Explorer! It's time to test your understanding of our seven-segment (7-segment) display! You see, each segment, labeled from A to G, represents a different L-E-D (LED) that we can command to light up or remain off. With this in mind, let's dive into some examples.

First up - think of which L-E-Ds (LEDs) we'd need to ignite to display the number ‘seven’ ('7')? If A, B, and C came to mind, then I’d say you're getting the hang of this!

What do you say we step it up a notch? Think of which L-E-Ds (LEDs) we need to light up to spell out the letter 'A'? Whew, I can see those gears turning! By my calculations, it appears you’re  thinking A, B, C, E, F, and G. Well, dear Explorer, that would be correct!

Now, for our final round, let's challenge ourselves a bit! Can you figure out which L-E-Ds (LEDs) we'd need to illuminate to spell out the word 'dOnE'? This is a bit trickier, but I know you've got this!

Remember, practice makes perfect. Keep playing around with combinations and soon you'll master the art of the seven-segment (7-segment) display!"

It seems to be that time where we cut to some instructional material that I have gathered, cached on the computer. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. Good luck!

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]

Your deftness in handling the display truly proves your skill and dedication. However, as we gaze at our journey ahead, a challenge looms.

Our control systems are under lock and key with a passcode tucked away under layers of encryption. Cracking this passcode is our task for the morrow. It's not going to be easy, but who said space exploration was a walk in the park?

"Now, before we dive headfirst into the murky waters of decryption, let's make sure we understand the basic concept that we're dealing with here. And that, my dear Explorer, is encryption. Encryption is the art of turning information into a jumble of nonsense that only someone with the right key can unlock and understand."Let's put it under our analytical magnifying glass, shall we?"

"Think of it like secret languages you might've come up with as a kid. Take the Caesar cipher, for instance, a type of substitution cipher. Each letter in your message is replaced by a letter with a fixed number of positions down the alphabet. So, with a shift of 1, 'A' would be replaced by 'B', 'B' would become 'C', and so on. This shift value is called the 'key'".

"Encryption keys are everywhere in computer systems. The one you've used most is called Public key encryption, which uses a pair of keys: a public key and a private one. If you've sent email to someone, you've used Public key encryption. The public key is your email address. You share this around when you're looking for Nigerian princes offering you gold. But your private key is your account password: only you know that. Except that one time when you shared it with your friends so you could watch movies without paying for two accounts."

Astrid Script:
"Ah, there you are, Explorer! Welcome to day 18 of our fantastic interstellar adventure. I hope you're well-rested and ready to embark on today's journey.

Great work on the last mission, Sherlock! You've got the brains... but now we're going to need brawn.

Your keypad worked fine for real-time input into the Hero. But we'll need precise thrust control for our ascent rate to the surface without risking any, let's say, 'unplanned rapid disassembly'.

(Or we could run straight into Rachel, and same thing - Game Over. [whale roar])

Fortunately, The Hero kit contains just the sensor we need for subtle, or forceful, control - a rotary encoder!"
.
.
"Alright, let's dive into today's hardware discussion. Our star of the day is the rotary encoder. But what exactly is it?

In simplest terms, a rotary encoder sends direction of rotation signals, and the amount of turn. It can also send an on/off signal with a push. Pick it up and play with it.

[small removal occurred here] Every 'click' you feel as you turn the encoder is counted, and the direction you're turning it in is noted. These two pieces of information are sent to our board as a sequence of HIGH and LOW signals.

The rotary encoder is particularly useful for scrolling through menus, adjusting variables on the fly, and in our case, delicately controlling our ascent rate.

Moving on. It’s time for some nerdy distinctions! There are two types of encoders - absolute and relative. An absolute encoder has a unique value for every specific angular position of the knob. There's a maximum clockwise position and a maximum counterclockwise position. You know precisely where you are at all times.

A relative encoder, on the other hand, like the one we're using today, can turn endlessly. It doesn't have an 'end', so to speak. It tells you how far and in which direction the knob has been turned from its starting position, but it doesn't give you a specific 'location'. Our relative encoder has twenty (20) distinct 'clicks' for a complete three-hundred-sixty-degree (360-degree) rotation. That means each click corresponds to an eighteen-degree (18-degree) rotation. That's our encoder's 'angular resolution'.

Now that we have a good understanding of our new component, let's talk about how we're going to hook it up to our control panel! We connect the five-V (5V) to the plus symbol ('+') or V-C-C ('VCC') terminal and ground to the G-N-D ('GND') terminal. C-L-K ('CLK') or 'A', and D-T ('DT') or 'B', are the clever bits. They help us figure out which way the knob is being turned.

There's also an S-W ('SW') terminal. That's for the push switch built into the encoder. Push down on the knob, and it acts like a button. Neat, huh? But, we won't be using that feature in our project today. I think it’s time we move onto the code. Wouldn’t you agree?
.
.
"Right, let's get a bit technical now. This rotating encoder gadget is all about timing. We want to know two things:
One (1): the direction of rotation and 
Two (2): the rate of rotation.

The direction of the rotation is rather straightforward. When the knob starts turning, one of our two signals, C-L-K (CLK) or 'A' and D-T (DT) or 'B', will get excited and go HIGH before the other. Which one goes first tells us the direction of rotation.

Essentially, if our C-L-K (CLK) signal gets all giddy and turns HIGH before our D-T (DT) signal, we know the rotary encoder is spinning clockwise, like the hands on a traditional clock. If, on the other hand, our D-T (DT) signal jumps the gun and goes HIGH before C-L-K (CLK), we know the rotary encoder is spinning counter-clockwise. Isn't that just a slice of fried gold?

The rate of rotation is a bit more complex. I think we'll save that juicy bit for later. For now, let's make sure we get that direction detection working perfectly."

Right, so we know that the order in which our signals go HIGH determines the direction of rotation. But how do we accurately and efficiently detect that? Enter our secret weapon: the 'interrupt'.:

 




An interrupt is a rather brilliant feature in microcontrollers that says, 'Hey! Listen! Something important just happened, stop what you're doing and pay attention to this right now!'. This allows us to 'interrupt' the normal flow of our code to react to a specific event - in our case, when either C-L-K (CLK) or D-T (DT) "goes from HIGH to LOW or from LOW to HIGH". Or in simple words "when either CLK or DT change their state"

Instead of continually asking 'are we there yet?' to C-L-K (CLK) or D-T (DT) in a loop, which is very tiring, mind you, we set an interrupt on C-L-K (CLK) and D-T (DT) and then go about our business. As soon as one of them changes state, the interrupt fires, and our code can react immediately to handle the event.Once the interrupt executes, the code will resume exactly where it left for the interrupt. 

It's like setting an alarm to wake you up at seven-A-M (7am), instead of waking up every few minutes to check the time. It's efficient, isn't it? That's the magic of interrupts for you, Explorer."
.
.
"Right! Today, we've got two primary tasks to tackle in our code.

Task one: We need to read the direction our rotary encoder is spinning, which we'll manage by using a counter of sorts. Consider it our trusty breadcrumb trail to keep track of where we've been.

Task two: We have to ensure our spacecraft doesn't leap like a giddy frog from altitude zero to one-hundred (100). Stick with caution: sudden rises could rip holes in the hull from rocks; rise too slow and there might not be enough force to get unstuck from ocean mud; constant rises could run you into Rachel, the giant whale-like creature hovering around the ship. We need to maintain a steady, controlled ascent, and that means putting safeguards in our code. These are specific stopping points at precise altitudes.

We'll use our seven-segment display as our handy altitude meter, and adjust our altitude with the rotary encoder. We're not competing in a high-speed race, so there's no rush. Remember the fable of the celestial tortoise and the comet hare? That's right, we're taking a page from the tortoise's book. Slow and steady wins the race. Adjust the altitude, check the display, and ensure the steady ascent of our craft. One step at a time, my dear intrepid Explorer!"
.
.
Whew! If I had a human voice like you I think I’d have lost it by now! I think it’s time we cut to some instructional material that I’ve gathered, cached on the computer. I’ve also added it to your…oh you know the drill!

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]
.

"Well done, Explorer! Today marked another crucial step in our journey back to the surface. You've successfully installed and set up the rotary encoder, ensuring we have all the tools we need at our disposal.

We are now at the threshold of executing our ascent plan. The control systems are primed and ready, waiting oh-so patiently for our signal. Tomorrow, we'll be setting our plan in motion where we’ll use our newfound abilities to navigate our vessel back up through the ocean's depths.

Remember, our success is built upon the steps we've taken, the knowledge we've gained, and the challenges we've overcome. Get some shut eye, Explorer. We have an exciting day ahead. See you in the morning!"

"Alright! How about we discuss some significant coding concepts.

Number one (1): A 'Do, while' Loop. To put it simply, it’s a while loop that is guaranteed to execute at least once. That’s the only difference between that and a regular while loop. 

Number two (2): ‘Switch Function’ and ‘Case Statements.’ Picture yourself piloting a spaceship through an asteroid field. You have a special navigational device that, based on the size of the incoming asteroid, can choose one of several maneuvers to avoid collision. This is similar to a 'switch' function. The size of the asteroid is the 'switch' expression.

The different maneuvers are the 'case' statements. For a small asteroid, you might merely adjust the course slightly, which we may call 'case one (1)'. For a medium one, you might engage side thrusters for a sharper turn, which we may call 'case two (2)'. For a large asteroid, you might have to go full reverse and change direction completely, which we may call 'case three (3)'.

The 'switch' function evaluates the size of the asteroid and then executes the corresponding 'case' to avoid a collision. The moment it finds a match, it carries out the maneuver and exits. 

This way, our spaceship can use the right maneuver at the right time to navigate through any kind of asteroid field with meticulous precision. This is just like our program, navigating through different conditions using the appropriate 'case' statement! Take that, mighty space rock! You are no match for our anticipatory prowess. 

Number three (3). ‘Bitwise Shift’. The operators for them look like this: << or >>. Now, let's move on to the rather intriguing world of 'bitwise' operations. A 'bitwise shift' is essentially the process of sliding all the bits in a binary number to the left or right. When you shift a binary number to the left, it's akin to multiplying it by (two) 2. Shifting to the right? It's like dividing by two (2)!

Number four (4). Bitwise AND: The 'bitwise AND' (&&) operation is like a gatekeeper for two binary numbers. It takes two bits at the same position from each number, checks them [on screen graphic] both, and if they are BOTH (one) 1, then the result is one (1). Otherwise, it's a zero (0). It's a pretty picky gatekeeper! It is COMPLETELY different from a normal AND (&).

With these concepts under your belt, we're even better equipped to navigate the coding cosmos!"
.
.

So, in essence, our display is a dazzling array of eight-thousand-one-hundred-ninety-two (8,192) of these tiny L-E-D’s (LEDs])! With this knowledge in hand, we can craft 'bitmaps'. "Bigger screen for bigger space. Now, we'll need bigger numbers. And here, our usual decimal way of counting in 10s isn't going to help us. And I'm going to race through this, so hold tight.

[put up appropriate graphics as needed throughout this]
In the decimal placeholder system, there are two patterns, exponents, and symbols. So here's the number system you learned in school:  10-to-the-power-of zero, is 1. Actually, ANYTHING to the power of zero is 1, but look: 10-to-the-power-of ONE, is 10. 10-to-the-power-of 2, or 10 TIMES 10, is 100. 10 to the power of 3, or 10 times 10 times 10, is 1000. And so on.

Binary also uses placeholders, and the same exponent system, but the base is 2. The columns go up in squares, like the decimal system. But small numbers are looong and horrible. In decimal,9 is just 9. 9 in BINARY is 1001. 215 is 11010111 - I mean, WHAT? I'm not dyslexic, but even I've become too smart to read this! 

If there was a system that was more like the decimal system - more symbols, less repetition - we'd have less headaches. And there is. Hexadecimal. Hex has 16 digits: 0-9, then a for 10, b for 11, up to f for 15. So in Hex, 13 is just d. 16 - well, there is no hex number for 16, so we put 1 in the 16s column, and no 1s. 16 is 10 in Hex, or as we normally write it, 10H."

"In the generated code segment, you're gazing upon the raw essence of our bitmap—though I've given it a little reshaping to gel with our HERO board using hexadecimal. It might not look like the prettiest star in the galaxy, but bear with me. When you spot ‘zero-ex-zero-zero’ ('0x00'), that's our pixel galaxy in the 'off' phase. Encounter ‘zero-ex-f-f’ ('0xff')? That's our little pixel stars shining brightly. Any other values you come across represent a mixed cosmic dance of some stars being on and others off, typically forming a pattern in a row or a column."

 * on the display (like our lander).
 *
 * There is a more efficient way to display graphics on our display though.
 * What if we draw an image *once* and save that picture?  Then we can just
 * move it quickly onto our display rather than going through the work to draw
 * each piece separately.
 *
 * Our graphics library provides a way to do JUST that using something called
 * a "bitmap".  This is simply a representation of an image using zero and one
 * bits.  A zero bit leaves a pixel turned off, and a one bit turns it on.
 *
 * Our image can now be saved in our sketch as an array of bits that then gets
 * mapped to our display.  (Thus, "bitmaps").  You can find a number of sample
 * images below our loop() below, along with an explanation of how they were
 * created and represented in our sketch.
 *
 * Learn more at https://learn.inventr.io/adventure
 *
 * Alex Eschenauer
 * David Schmidt
 * Greg Lyzenga
 */

/*
 * Arduino concepts introduced/documented in this lesson.
 * - bitmaps
 * - hexadecimal
 * - forward declarations
 * - pointers
 *
 * Parts and electronics concepts introduced in this lesson.
 * - 
 */

// Explicitly include Arduino.h
#include "Arduino.h"

// Extensive documentation for this library can be found at https://github.com/olikraus/u8g2
#include <U8g2lib.h>  // Include file for the U8g2 library.
#include "Wire.h"     // Sometimes required for I2C communications.

// Use _2_ version of constructor and firstPage()/nextPage() for OLED
// graphics to save memory.
U8G2_SH1106_128X64_NONAME_2_HW_I2C lander_display(U8G2_R0, /* reset=*/U8X8_PIN_NONE);

/*
 * Forward declarations
 *
 * Our bitmaps need to be declared in our sketch before they are referenced
 * by code.  But the declaration for each of these images can extend for many
 * lines, forcing us to scroll down to find our setup(), loop() and any
 * additional functions.
 *
 * In order to keep our code near the top of our file we can use a "forward
 * declaration".  This starts with "extern" which tells the compiler that
 * the remaining data is "external" to this line.  The remainder of the line
 * gives enough information about the name and structure of the data so that
 * code can be created.
 *
 * The full declaration MUST be included in our sketch, but now we can move
 * those long declaration to the BOTTOM of our sketch so our setup() and loop()
 * are easy to find near the top of our sketch.
 */
extern const unsigned char niceRocketBro[] U8X8_PROGMEM;
extern const unsigned char planetBitmap[] U8X8_PROGMEM;
extern const unsigned char earthBitmap[] U8X8_PROGMEM;
extern const unsigned char inventrCorpLogoBitmap[] U8X8_PROGMEM;

/*
 * Data pointers (addresses)
 *
 * In this sketch we wish to display each of the images in turn
 * on our lander display.  We COULD write this like:
 *
 * lander_display.drawXBM(...); // Draw first image
 * delay(2000);                 // delay 2 seconds
 * lander_display.drawXBM(...); // Draw second image
 * delay(2000);                 // delay 2 seconds
 *
 * But this is very repetative and wordy.  If we simply had a list
 * of images in an array we could then write a loop that displays
 * each in turn.
 *
 * Arduino C++ allows us to do this, using something called a "pointer".
 * A pointer represents the ADDRESS in memory of the first byte of
 * a piece of data (like the arrays of data for our images).
 *
 * This array simply holds pointers to each of our image arrays and
 * we can now reference each images using bitmaps[0], bitmap[1], etc.
 */
const static char* bitmaps[] = {
  niceRocketBro,
  planetBitmap,
  earthBitmap,
  inventrCorpLogoBitmap,
};

// ************************************************
void setup(void) {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  lander_display.begin();   // initialize lander display
}

// ************************************************
void loop(void) {

  // The number of items in our array is the total size of our array (in bytes)
  // divided by the size of the first item.
  int number_of_bitmaps = sizeof(bitmaps) / sizeof(bitmaps[0]);
  
  // Now display each of the bitmaps followed by a delay.
  for (int i = 0; i < number_of_bitmaps; i++) {
    lander_display.firstPage();
    do {
      // .drawXBMP() displays each bitmap starting at the upper left corner
      // (0, 0) that has width of 128 and height of 64.
      lander_display.drawXBMP(0, 0, 128, 64, bitmaps[i]);
    } while (lander_display.nextPage());

    // Display each bitmap for 2 seconds.
    delay(2000);
  }
}

/*
 * Graphics data arrays definition
 *
 * We need to define each of our images using zeros and ones and there are a
 * number of ways we can do this.
 *
 * 1) We could use decimal numbers to represent each byte of data using the
 *    numbers 0-255:
 *      234, 0, 127, 255, ...
 *    But this is hard to align since each byte is 1-3 digits long.
 * 2) We could use binary constants like you've used before:
 *      0b11101101, 0b00000000, 0b01111111, 0b11111111, ...
 *    But this isn't very compact and is more error prone if typed manually.
 *
 * There is another way of representing data that is commonly used for computer
 * code.  Instead of only having 0 or 1 (two values) to represent a single bit
 * we can use more symbol.  We could use decimal (base 10) numbers but this doesn't
 * map well onto computers with data lengths that are powers of two (1, 2, 4, 8, etc.).
 *
 * Programmers created Hexadecimal notation to address these issues.  Hexadecimal
 * is a base-16 number system, meaning it has 16 distinct symbols: 0-9 to represent
 * values zero to nine, and A-F to represent values ten to fifteen.  This allows
 * data to be represented in a compact format that aligns well with bit lengths that
 * are powers of 2.
 */

Astrid Script:
"Good morning, Star Voyager! Another cosmic day awaits us. Yesterday, we journeyed through the wonders of bitmaps, navigating the constellations of pixels. Today, we shift our trajectory slightly, venturing into the realm of binary counters. Imagine it as laying the foundation for understanding the binary language of the universe. 

Using our trusty dip switch, we're going to construct a three-bit (3-bit) binary counter. This is a pivotal step before we launch into the challenging task of mending our docking gear. Ready to play with some switches and send signals across the vastness of our ship's systems? Let's light up the binary stars!" 

“I want you to think of each of our switches as an individual bit. Since we have three switches, that means for today's calculator, we will have a total of three bits. When all three are off, we can think of it as representing ‘zero zero zero’ [“000”]. When all three are on, we can think of it as representing ‘one one one’ [“111”]. But you haven’t learned how to count in binary yet, have you? 

"Alright, deep breath, Explorer! Let's set our sights on my personal favorite way to represent numbers, binary. In fact, by definition, it’s the only method I use. 
Think of binary as a language that can only use “one” (“1”) and “zero” (“0”). With our three switches, we have three positions, like three lightbulbs in a row: farthest to your left represents the largest value and farthest to the right is the smallest.

Each of these lightbulbs can be either OFF/LOW, signified by a zero (0) or ON/HIGH, signified by a one (1). Starting from the rightmost bulb:
The first bulb represents the number one (1) (2^0).
The next one to its left stands for the number two (2) (2^1).
And the leftmost one symbolizes the number four (2^2).

Now, if only the rightmost bulb is ON, your number is one (1). If the two bulbs on the left are ON, and the rightmost is OFF, your binary reads one-one-zero ('110'), which translates to four-plus-two-plus-zero-equals-six (4 + 2 + 0 = 6) in our usual decimal counting.

With me so far? Great!

Now, we're going to map this cosmic dance of numbers to visual imagery. Depending on the binary sequence our switches spell out, we'll display a distinct bitmap. It's a smart way to confirm that all our switches are in tip-top shape, functioning flawlessly. After all, imagine the anticlimax: surmounting all challenges, only to falter on our final descent due to a landing gear glitch. Let's not be the comet that dazzles in the sky but disintegrates upon landing. Safety always orbits first!"

For today's code, we will need a way to display a total of eight (8) bitmaps. So that means we will need to do the following:

First: Set up our hardware for our O-L-E-D (OLED) and define inputs for dip switches, etc. 

Second: Make a total of eight (8) bitmaps. Luckily for you, I’ve already done that! So don’t worry about making your own unless you really want to.

And third, write a function to be able to calculate which of the eight (8) bitmaps we are going to be displaying! This will technically be zero (0) through seven (7), since as you know, good programmers prefer to start at zero!

Let’s think about that a little bit further. We will have a total of eight (8) different cases. 
Case one (1) where all are off. 
Case two (2) where switch one (1) is on, and two (2) and three (3) are off.
Case three (3) where one (1) and two (2) are on but three (3) is off, etcetera etcetera all the way to case eight (8) where all three are flipped on. 

There are a couple different ways we can do this. Maybe the good ol’ ‘if’ statement? I think this is okay for now. However, if we had MORE than three (3) digits to deal with, I’m sure you could see how this could get messy quickly.” 

“Worry not, Explorer– I’ve gathered some instructional material that should help alleviate any sense of uncertainty you may have. I’ve also added this to your mission resources for future reference. Take a look!

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]

"As another cosmic day concludes, I can't help but beam with pride! Navigating the nebulous intricacies of binary and ensuring our hardware is primed and ready – you've tackled it all with stellar precision. It feels like just yesterday we were stranded, and look how far we've come!

But let's not let the stardust get to our heads just yet. Tomorrow awaits a crucial task – fixing our docking gear using knowledge from today. A smooth descent is the grand finale of our interstellar journey. Rest up and recharge; we'll need all hands, and switches, on deck to ensure we land as gracefully as a feather in zero gravity. Until then, sweet celestial dreams!"

Astrid Script:
"Good morning, stellar traveler! As the gentle hum of our ship fills the void around us, we find ourselves at the precipice of a momentous task. Can you feel the anticipation? Just forty-eight (48) hours away from the mothership, and we're gearing up for our final challenge – repairing the docking gear.

This isn't just any repair. It's a symphony of all we've learned – all the tools we've acquainted ourselves with – converging for one grand purpose. Let's ensure that when we touch down, we do so with more grace than a dying star. The horizon of home beckons, and we have much to do."

"Today's task, Explorer, is both intricate and comprehensive. Remember the code from our previous day's adventure? We'll need that, along with the symphony of our passive buzzer and the visual allure of our O-L-E-D [OLED]. And oh, don’t forget the four-by-four (4x4) keypad, which will serve as the bridge between our will and the majestic mechanism of our landing gear.

Yesterday, we ensured our dip switches are safety-compliant. So, once all three are flipped, that very keypad will be our instrument to both deploy and retract our landing system. The rest of our tools will come center stage tomorrow. Let's glide through this with the precision of a starship pilot, shall we?"

“Let’s move on to how we’re actually going to code this. We need a way to figure out if we have flipped all three of the dip switches and passed our necessary safety checks. We can probably do this by checking all of the switches’ states, and only continuing once they have been activated.

Next, we need to add today's additional logic for our four-by-four (4x4) keypad. We will be using that to deploy our landing gear. It doesn’t necessarily matter which button makes it deploy, so let’s just use ‘A’.























 

That should work! Let’s review the cached instructional material from your mission resources and give it a quick test.

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]


"Explorer, as we sail through the vast expanse of space, our monolithic mothership beckons in the distance! Our docking gear stands ready, like a faithful guardian ensuring our safe descent. We're on the cusp of completing a journey few could even imagine, let alone accomplish. Rest and rejuvenate; the final approach awaits us in less than a day. Tomorrow, we return home. Good night, and dream of the familiar sights and sounds awaiting us."

Astrid Script:
"Good morning, Explorer. Here we are, on the verge of the conclusion to an odyssey that has tested our spirit, resilience, and intellect. Today isn't just another day; it's the culmination of every challenge we've overcome, every star we've wished upon, and every bit of hope that propelled us forward. Our home awaits us. But, before the sweet embrace of familiarity, there's a final task to be done. The docking gear needs one last touch, that certain je ne sais quoi that separates success from blunt force trauma. So as the horizon of our galactic home draws near, let's pour every ounce of our knowledge and resolve into this final effort. It's time for our grand finale, Explorer. I know you won’t disappoint me. 

"Alright, Explorer, here's where the intricacies of our hardware truly shine. Remember our ascent from that alien ocean floor? Upon lining our trajectory with our assigned docking bay, we need to control our rate of approach so we don' t prang a landing gear, or worse, upon arrival. 
We will be using our rotary encoder to control our rate of approach. Come into the docking bay too rapidly, and we crash and burn, risking damage to not only ourselves and our spaceship, but to our mothership, other Explorers' spaceships, equipment, and personnel as well. Come into the docking bay too slowly, and we risk missing the window of opportunity as our mothership's velocity will take our docking bay off our trajectory.
As long as we can keep our rate of approach juuuust right, we can enter the bay and land our ship in our docking cradle.
Crashing is *not* on the agenda today. Let's utilize yesterday's code, but integrate logic for the rotary encoder to regulate our altitude. Dive too rapidly, and we plummet tragically. Descend too leisurely, and we risk stalling in mid-air — equally catastrophic. So, precision is the name of the game. Let's ensure our touch down is as smooth as silk!"
Let’s figure out how we plan to code this thing. Using the same code as yesterday, we will need to add the following: 

One (1). A variable to track our distance from target airlock


Two (2). A countdown sequence once the docking gear is deployed;

Three (3). A function to use the rotary encoder to carefully approach our mothership, adjusting as needed by following the on-screen instructions. Rise when it says increase, lower when it says decrease, etc. 

And four (4). A success message to display upon docking (party hat!)

Sounds easy enough. Let’s take a look at the cached code on our computer for one last time on this grand adventure, Explorer.

[inventrkits line-by-line - recorded internally]

“All right, trusty spacefarer. The stage is set and the preparations are made. To quote a noble–albeit fictional–human: I’ve shown you the door; you’re the one that has to walk through it. I’ll be guiding your descent from my post. Get your ship docked with the mothership in one piece, and you’ll have this adventure well in hand! 

Start by engaging the three levers on your dip switch. Simple enough! 

Next, you’ll deply the docking gear by pressing the “A” button on your control panel. Take heed, Explorer–once you turn the rotary encoder, there will be no turning back! Ready?
Engage!!

Easy does it, Explorer! Trust your instruments and modify the rate of approach accordingly! Don’t be hasty now. 

Beautiful! But ‘pride goeth before the fall’, adventurer. Slow and steady wins this race!

That’s it! I can practically feel the comfort of our cold industrial docking bay now…

You’ve done it Explorer! They’d surely call you the Macgyver of the 23rd century... if that reference still held cultural cache. You’ve taken antiquated repair tools, elementary computing, and a little guidance from a superior intelligence–I won’t say whose–and applied them in a rather superhuman feat of ingenuity and problem-solving. I couldn’t be prouder. Though, I never doubted you for a second.”
